abstract:
When Nana Osei-Opare approached me about reprinting my article, “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa,” I initially hesitated. Written when I was only twenty-three for a graduate seminar at UCLA, the piece was a preliminary research project based on a narrow range of primary sources—a Constraint born of the fact that South African archives were then inaccessible to me. At the time, I had embarked on an ambitious, perhaps unrealistic, dissertation comparing the Black Left in South Africa and the U.S. South. Given that President P. W. Botha had declared a state of emergency in 1985, and my own involvement in protests at the South African Consulate in Beverly Hills likely precluded a visa, the prospect of conducting field research seemed dim.

Revisiting the essay three decades later, I find parts of it cringeworthy, particularly its occasional lapses into rigid Marxist jargon. Furthermore, the Soviet Union was still years away from collapse, meaning the Comintern archives remained closed. Yet, considering these limitations and the sheer audacity of youth, elements of the work still resonate. Drawing inspiration from Cedric Robinson’s *Black Marxism*, which had been published just years prior in 1983, I argued that a Black radical tradition—informed by African redemptionism, rural resistance to the 1913 Land Act, and emerging African nationalism—had already articulated a vision of self-determination. These indigenous movements predated the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA), which initially focused on the white working class. Indeed, it is jarring to recall a Communist Party supporting strikers under the slogan, “Workers of the World Unite and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” It was only when African trade unionists and Comintern officials in Moscow challenged this narrow focus on the 1922 Rand Revolt that the CPSA began to account for the struggles of the far more numerous African miners.
